item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: Emergency Podcast: The Iran War Impact on Vietnam | Michael Kokalari | EP 384

Transcript

**** · 20 million barrels of oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, which [music] has been disrupted. That's about 17% of global oil demand. Prices went up 4x.

**** · Trump is about to address. He'll probably be looking for off-ramps or some way to back out of the big mistake that he did. But if he doesn't back out within the next [music] 2 to 3 weeks, the economy will suffer a hit which is bigger than COVID. You say worse than COVID.

**** · Yeah.

**** · A lot of people did quite well. We will have a difficult hit to the economy [music] this year. Fertilizer prices have more or less doubled. Gold prices are going to go up dramatically. It's going to be over 6,000 by the end of the year.

**** · It's a very unfortunate situation that [music] the people in the upper middle class, they're okay. And then the people, the working [music] people are the ones that suffer. In difficult times this, we may have to really, all of us, come together and push through this.

**** · [music] It's very important that people know Vietnam is going to come out of this in a much stronger position than people realize. [music] I want to go back to an acronym that you used earlier, TACO. Trump always chickens out. Good morning, Vietnam. Today, we have a special guest, a special episode. It's an emergency Vietnam Innovators podcast. It's with our good friend Mike Kokalari, the chief economist of Inner Capital.

**** · Just last night, it depends when we're releasing this, but last night he wrote to myself and the team and sent us a note about the oil prices. what's going on and what's going to happen to the economy globally, but also specifically Vietnam. And the last podcast, if you guys recall that we did with Mike, you guys had a lot of feedback wanting to hear more about the future of Vietnam's economy, its role as a connector economy. A lot of different comments and we're going to address as many of those as possible today in relation to what's going on because a lot of interesting things happening in the world and we want Mike, one of the few people here in Vietnam who's super excited to talk about it almost every day. I think that's his job.

**** · Passion. That's his passion. Job and passion. Mike, welcome back to the studio. Thank you for having me.

**** · [music] As you mentioned, we published a report on Maybe we'll put the link or something on our this website. But maybe I start by just giving a little summary of the topics that we discussed in the report. And then, you do your thing and see where you want to go with that after.

**** · So, people will know that are, reading the newspaper and whatnot, that 20 million barrels of oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, which has been disrupted. That's about 17% of global oil demand. Oil prices, I think everybody knows, are up around 70 80% now. Interestingly, if you look at what happened in the 1970s, it was around 6 or 7% of supply that was disrupted, much lower number, but oil prices went up 4x.

**** · So, part of the reason why oil price did not go up as much is this anticipation that Trump is going to back off. you hear this term people say TACO, Trump always chickens out. I have a term I call TACO paradox. Maybe we'll get into that. As we're filming this, Trump is about to address the US He'll probably be looking for off-ramps or some way to back out of this big mistake that he did, to be honest. But if he doesn't back out within the next 2 to 3 weeks, if he doesn't back out the economy will suffer a hit which is bigger than COVID. And Asia in particular is going to have a really difficult time cuz so much flows through the Strait. It's not just oil, but it's obviously fertilizer, helium, LNG, this stuff. Now, that having been said, in Vietnam, the government has done a pretty good job in navigating the situation. We think that Vietnam would be okay and told maybe the middle of May, late May, something that. The prime minister visited Russia last week where he secured probably another 1 or 2 weeks' worth of oil.

**** · China has very adept in dishing out some oil and refined products in the region. That's probably another 2 or 3 weeks' worth. So, one of the things I will tell people who are not in Vietnam is that when you're out and about, you don't see any sign of that. it's not there's lines for the petrol.

**** · That's not here.

**** · In the Philippines, they've declared a national emergency. Other countries in the region, they've shut down schools, etc. etc. So far, we don't have any of that. Cuz the government, I think, people feel comfortable that the government knows how to navigate this.

**** · And the very last thing I want to say in my little intro is that we will have a difficult hit to the economy this year from all of this. But what lies ahead for Vietnam on the other side of this, it won't be good for the world, but in a way, it will be good for Vietnam. And I know that sometimes I get accused of being overly optimistic, saying too bright of a future cuz I really believe in this country. But I'm not the only one who's talking about this ideas. And you said it in the intro of what people are calling connector countries. Some people call ball bearing countries. So, these tectonic plates are shifting.

**** · But you Vietnam, because of the bamboo diplomacy, we have very good relations with the US and the friend shoring countries. We also have very relations with the BRIC countries. So, there was some IMF report last year to this effect. Mark Carney gave a very famous speech at Davos recently. And people talk about countries Vietnam, Mexico, Poland, Singapore that are be able to benefit from, this tectonic shift. So, that's the topics. And you're probably the best interviewer I've ever encountered in my career. So, I would say from there, let's just jump off and whatever you think the viewers will find interesting.

**** · Mike, it's in the morning as we're filming this on a weekday and I'm just imagining you in your crisp suit [laughter] on a sale on my motorbike just flying over here.

**** · I I also want to share to the audience Mike is not only genuinely passionate about this stuff, but he wants to share. So, we're so excited to have you on the program today. I want to start with one big question because even my team is asking. The everyday people I speak with at work and going out, they're all thinking the same thing, too.

**** · Who gets affected the most in Vietnam because of this?

**** · Is it the farmers? Is it the consumers?

**** · Is it the business businesses? who's who's going to feel the most heat in the next few weeks? It's a very unfortunate situation that there's an economic phenomenon in the US that we talked a lot about, a lot of people talk about, called the K-shaped economy in which the people in the upper middle class, they're okay. And then the people, the working people are the ones that suffer.

**** · In Vietnam, we talked about that topic a lot in the US. And now in Vietnam, you hear people talk about that here as well. And so, unfortunately, I think, fertilizer prices have more or less doubled. this is really going to cause difficulty among working people. And for that reason, I think what you'll see, I expect that the government will take much more In the past, when they subsidized petrol prices here it never amounted to more than half a percent of GDP. And that compares to something 3% of GDP in Indonesia.

**** · So, I think you'll see the government taking much more aggressive steps to help that the bottom part of the K in the economy here. one of the most famous things about Vietnam everyday life is that it's generally affordable. Now, it could go up as well, but it's generally affordable for every everyone, everyday people, upper middle class, anyone. But I want to break it down a little bit, especially for our audience here.

**** · for someone that goes into their convenience store each morning and buys a coffee for 15,000. **** · Is that cost expected to Absolutely.

**** · what increase are you are you Yeah. That's a great question. What the government did last week is they went in and they directly lopped about 20% off of petrol prices using, cutting taxes and this stuff. So, that's good. But diesel prices are up almost 100%.

**** · So, that coffee's transported to the to the store by diesel. fertilizer accounts for about 20% of food cost. So, that's, as I said, it's almost doubled. So, you can do some back of the envelope calculation.

**** · inflation is headed to over 5% unless they really take some dramatic actions. And so, that's, the people who get hit by inflation the most are the ones that are, living off of the salary because the salary does not go up, away to compensate them for that. Yeah, I think businesses, everyone's going to figure out how to adapt. And Yeah. that's going to be the big question for us. I want to talk about the impact that conflicts have on economies because, my generation, your generation, the people that are living today, we generally had a peaceful existence.

**** · Compared to our parents and grandparents, perhaps not.

**** · I suspect you're trying to set me up for the gold question again.

**** · Yeah.

**** · [laughter] So, but Yeah. the question is if this conflict continues Yeah. and especially the Strait of Hormuz, even though Vietnam's not involved, continues to constrain supply.

**** · What do you think are the most universal things in terms of economic impact that countries will face in the long term?

**** · Well, you and I talk about this and also sometimes offline as well that gold prices are going to go up dramatically. What you see so far, and this is not uncommon, is that initially gold price goes down because it's the only thing people can sell, Which is what happened the last time.

**** · And this is exactly what happened. And so many of my Vietnamese friends are panicking "Oh, should I get out?" And I'm telling them, "Absolutely not. It's going to be over 6,000 by the end of the year."

**** · How about Bitcoin?

**** · Yeah, [snorts] Bitcoin went up around 3 or 4%. I'm I'm less of a more of a gold expert than a Bitcoin expert.

**** · alternative assets.

**** · Alternative assets, yeah. Because the only solution for Western governments to all this will be to print a lot more money. So that's one consequence. But the more immediate consequence, I gave you a little bit of a dramatic statement in the opening that this will be worse than COVID because all these ships that are, have left the Middle East, they're all arriving now as we speak. And just everything is going to run out.

**** · Let's take a step back. Let's go back four or five six years ago. Yeah. You say worse than COVID. A lot of people did quite well. Yeah. The stock market hit record highs at the time.

**** · That's I believe, at least the upper middle class did quite well. Anyone that had resources to allocate Okay. So investments Let me ask you a question. Back to the interviewer. Yeah. How much do you think Vietnam's oil consumption, it obviously had to go down cuz we had pretty severe lockdowns. Take a guess. Just a wild guess out of the air.

**** · What do you think it is?

**** · Okay, I'm going to give you the number and then I'm going to explain why I Okay. Okay. It has to be at least, I would say, 30%.

**** · Yeah. Everyone guesses 30, 40, 50, which is a very normal guess. It only went down by around 15%. 15 is our estimate.

**** · So I'll I'll tell you why I thought, "Okay, 15 sounds a little bit reasonable." It's cuz the number of motorbikes just going around delivering things. There's a bit of a joke in the business community that you have to stamp and sign papers.

**** · At any given moment there's 5% of motorbikes on the road just delivering paperwork. So that alone So the point that I'm trying to lead into is that Vietnam is a very energy-intensive economy. The energy intensiveness of Vietnam's economy is twice the Philippines, almost three times Indonesia. And is that And that explains why our oil consumption did not go very go down very much. So the point I'm trying to lead into is what if it's imposed on us from the outside? What if we because of these global events, you have to cut by 30%. Well, then you're going to see massive contraction in the GDP. And that's why I say it would be worse than COVID. I have another question, which is the sources of oil. You mentioned Russia and China are giving some temporary relief. Vietnam has its sources as well.

**** · And then but and you mentioned 17% of global supply goes through the Middle East. How much from that area is going to Vietnam? Yeah, okay.

**** · This is a Is it meaningful or is it just because all the other countries are demand skyrocketing so Vietnam is a collateral impact?

**** · a great question. I'm going to answer it in a little bit different way than you posed the question. Our oil consumption is around 500 bit more than 500,000 barrels a day in Vietnam. To put that in context, that's similar to South Africa, countries that. And we produce here almost 200,000. So we had the back wall field that's, gradually the production of that's going down 10% a year.

**** · We have two oil refineries, as **** · So we produce about 70% of all the refined products that we use, gasoline, diesel, this stuff. But here's the thing. One of those refineries was essentially financed by the Kuwaitis, the Kuwaitis and the Japanese. And so in Vietnam we produce what's called light oil, but that refinery requires heavy oil. So it turns out that a very high percentage of our oil is sourced from the Middle East. Yeah, that is the way to put it. And let's for the for the more folks that are not familiar with the all these terms. So oil is the product that you get off the ground.

**** · Yeah.

**** · And then the refineries, typically they could exist in the Middle East, but usually it's in the countries where they want to then use it. So Vietnam has that infrastructure. Yeah, we can satisfy about 70% of our own, yeah. So at some point By the way, one consequence of this conflict is every country around the world will build more refining capacity, And also renewable energy, we should view renewable energy from an energy security point of view.

**** · Let's talk about that cuz it's a bit prescient. I would say, just two three months ago we had the National Congress. At the National Congress, the Lam and all these other folks were mentioning how Vietnam wants, I think, to hit 50% of its energy source renewable.

**** · **** · Where is it today? and do you think they were thinking about independence and being less reliance on external sources at the time? Where it is today is sufficiently small that I don't know the number. Okay. So it's it's probably 10-ish or something that. And that's after having made a huge push for solar energy this year.

**** · happening now, obviously. The fact And I'll say something a bit cynical. In general, I really admire the government and I think that they've done a great job. And what I'm about to say also shows in a way that they've done a great job. The focus on renewables is to make foreign investors happy to invest here. This is And a very smart strategy, but this is what that's about.

**** · Okay.

**** · And who are those foreign investors? What why do they see Vietnam as an attractive place to invest for renewables?

**** · well it's definitely not the Americans, not at moment at least. Or I could be wrong about that.

**** · Well, okay. So to be clear what I'm saying, what I'm saying is that the push in renewables is to ensure steady investment. For example, the LEGO, they want they want the net zero energy. Our firm VinaCapital was instrumental in developing what's called the DPPA, direct power purchase agreement, where a factory can go directly to a solar energy farm and say, "we want to get your electricity from you so that we can tell our customers in the West we're net zero, "

**** · So it's a little bit different. It's it's a little bit different than I am at than what we Yeah, so it's say but let's take a step back from there. 10 years ago when me and you first Almost 20 years ago.

**** · But the number of let's say, office towers or manufacturing complexes and bases, they were operating in a way that was not necessarily energy efficient. And then you had this emergence of LEED certified That has exploded the demand for it. And as a result, the infrastructure has to follow. So that's But it's exploded and it's it's a bit cynical what I'm about to say now, but it's exploded because that's a prerequisite for making foreign investors want to It's it's a it's a checklist in the a corporate It's also good very good for the country, by the way, to be more energy efficient and more self-sufficient, sure. And less pollution, of course. I want to also take a look back a little bit about past history because everything we're sharing is a bit hypothetical, although it's grounded in facts, of course. But we can only guess what will happen. Yeah. we just said Trump is now doing an address to the nation US nation in a couple of hours or Trying to figure out some way to back down. Even whatever he says, we don't really know if it'll happen, which is fair enough. I know what is going to happen.

**** · [laughter] He's going to say, "I win." And then he's going to leave.

**** · Let's let's see and [music] So I want to take a look at that history a little bit. So my memory, although I was not born at the time, was back in 1979 you had the Iran hostage crisis. And as a result, there was an oil impact, at least on the US. Yeah. I'm I'm I'm not sure at all how the rest of the world was faring at the time. But could you could you give us a bit of a history lesson about oil shocks, especially this was also Iran in '79. What happened back then?

**** · Well, in both the '73 and '79, the volume of oil that was disrupted was something around 6, 7% of global '73, of course, is the most famous one cuz that was the first one. And oil price went up four times that Four times.

**** · Yeah.

**** · And where was all the oil being consumed at the time? Was that mostly the US and Europe? Huge proportion in the US. You got to remember that's before Alaska really came online or when it was just starting to come online.

**** · Yeah. And of course, in more recent this whole fracking oil phenomenon, the US is the biggest producer in the world now, around 13 million barrels a day, something that. And I want to address that point, too, cuz on Twitter or Truth Social, whatever it's called, Trump It should be it should be called Untruth Social.

**** · [laughter] Trump was saying something along the lines of how the US has its own energy sources. So the rest of the world, needs to figure it out themselves. Is that true? Yeah, it is. It is. So if you take a step back from the '70s to today, the energy sources for a lot of countries have diversified essentially.

**** · And at least the US has become very independent. Has its own refineries, not just sources, but refineries as well.

**** · How did the rest of the world fare in that time, '73, '79? Did also oil prices 4x that time? And what happened to the people? Cuz 4x, you said 80% is what we're facing today. And Much less, yeah. we're okay. In Vietnam, I don't I personally don't see too many things going on. But four times, what was the everyday life of a person when oil prices were 4x? Well, I'm older than you. So I will tell you Let me tell you Sure. Sure. we there were very long lines. You had to wait an hour to get gas. And your license plate if you depending on whether your license plate was even or odd, you were allowed to go to the gas station on different days.

**** · And the US suffered at that time what people call stagflation. So, which is a combination of huge inflation and and very, falling GDP growth. And that's a concern for the world now. People are concerned are we going to enter into a stagflationary era?

**** · at that time the US was a net importer of oil. After Alaska came online, let's say probably in the early 2010s, we produced around 4 or 5 million barrels a day. But then there was this whole fracking explo- explosion. Well, I use the word explosion. It and it's based on explosion. You put water into the well, you make an explosion, and then water and then oil leaks out of the rocks. And now the US produces is the biggest oil producer in the world, around 12 13 million barrels. And that's one of the reasons why Trump can be a little flip and say, I created this problem for Europe and now it's You see what Marco Rubio was on TV the one or two days ago.

**** · clip **** · Yeah, and he was saying well, we hope that the Iranians will reopen the Strait of Hormuz, but maybe they won't, And maybe they'll just end up If the Iranians end up charging $2 million a ship, they'll make as much money on the tolls as they will on the oil for them. So, I think that's the likely where this is going to go.

**** · Yeah. I think what's going to happen is the Strait will reopen, but the you'll be forced to pay a toll to the Iranians going forward. I wanted to also add a little bit more Vietnam context because in the news a couple days ago, I believe VN Express reported or whoever it was that the Ministry of Foreign Affairs had made a request or was pending a request Yeah. to the Iranians to allow Vietnamese flag-bearing ships to go through.

**** · **** · Do anything about that? What are your thoughts about that? That obviously would be very good for Vietnam if it could.

**** · Yeah.

**** · Is that going to happen even? I think it's not the first priority that the Iranians are thinking about.

**** · Yeah, fair enough. Yeah, they've got a lot of things going on. I think that All the countries are asking them probably Yeah, it's good that the government here, that's very smart, but I just think that's they're obligated to do that. things they're going to do now. There's a lot going on.

**** · Okay. I also want to compare Vietnam to the region a little bit. You mentioned the Philippines and others have school closures. they're encouraging work from home.

**** · Yeah. I saw something about Korea where the Korean Airlines have to slash 50% of frequencies. What is the status quo in Vietnam at the moment?

**** · Yeah.

**** · I So, I just came from a trip to Nha Trang a few days ago and I fly Vietnam Airlines and I always assume they have a flight to Nha Trang I don't know every 2 hours or something. But they did. They only had flights in the morning and then night at night. Nothing during the day. Yeah, about 20% of flights have been cut, now, which is smart. But other than that, we haven't really seen big major impacts. if you add up the amount of oil that the government holds in its SPR, strategic petroleum reserve, how much the refineries have on hand, how much PetroVietnam has on hand. Yeah. I think life can go on pretty much as normal until around mid-May, maybe late May without any noticeable disruption.

**** · And I think the whole war stuff is going to be over by then, to be honest.

**** · Yeah.

**** · Yeah. Got it. Mike, thanks for giving that analysis. I want to go back to an acronym that you used earlier, TACO. Yeah. which I forget what it stands for, but you're going to tell me in just a second. Trump always chickens out.

**** · Okay. And I didn't make that up.

**** · Everyone's saying that.

**** · But I added this little twist, the TACO paradox.

**** · **** · So, what does that mean exactly?

**** · Okay. So, the idea is that I mentioned that the oil market has not really gone up. it's gone up a lot, but nothing compared to '73. The stock market's come off and the stock market in Vietnam as well has come off, but not really that much if you consider that we could be facing something more severe than COVID. so, the question is why hasn't oil gone up more? And why hasn't the US stock market gone down more? And the answer is everyone's expecting Trump to back off or to do the TACO.

**** · However, in order for Trump to back off, the market has to crash first. So, there's a little circular thing here. Things haven't really fallen apart because they're expecting him to back off, but what we've seen in the past is he doesn't back off until, the markets really crash. And what people are looking at in particular is the 10-year yield on the US Treasury. So, when that goes Every time over the last month, every time the 10-year US yield has gone up to about the 4.5% level, then you see he's backed up. Initially, he said "I'm going to attack Iran in 48 hours." And as soon as the 10-year yield got to 4 and 1/2%, he said, "Well, I'll attack in 5 days. well, I'll attack in 10 days." so that seems to be his pain threshold. That's the same level, by the way, at which during the liberation, a year ago, as soon as the the 10-year got up to there, that means people's mortgages in the US are over 6%. So, if that happens, if the price of gasoline gets over 4 and 1/2 $5, then you'll see him back off. Okay. So, Yeah, the midterms in the US are approaching. So, he has a sense of urgency there.

**** · your report despite what you just shared today, very generally glowing, I would say. That's my perspective. And I agree with you. despite that, you say that it's the 10th most vulnerable nation globally. And also I just saw a clip on The Economist, I believe, that said something around those lines, too.

**** · So, I didn't say that. The economist Oh, so okay. Sorry, I'm getting confused.

**** · So, you quoted a source that said Vietnam is the 10th most So, the So, The Economist magazine said Vietnam is the 10th most. And The Economist magazine said that Vietnam's vulnerability is worse than our neighbors So, do you agree with that? No, I don't. And what I'll One thing I'll say is that the Standard Chartered and you can find it on Reuters and other places have said that we're not the 10th most.

**** · There's others that are including our regional neighbors. So, I have a two-part question. Why do you think The Economist and maybe other people, too, even ranked Vietnam 10th most in the first place? Number one. Number two is doesn't matter what the ranking is.

**** · Could be 10th, it could be 100th. What could Vietnam do to be less vulnerable in the future?

**** · well, the biggest thing is that we don't have precise data on that, but in our understanding from reading, comments of various government officials and whatnot, Vietnam only has about 10 days' worth of the strategic petroleum reserve. that's Thailand's is probably 10 times that.

**** · So, they will need to spend money in the future to build up a bigger SPR. And they'll certainly need to a topic we discussed earlier, build more refining capacity here. And I would encourage them to keep pushing the renewable energy agenda from an energy security point of view and also to help the pollution is great and make foreign investors feel more encouraged. SPR, interesting term. Strategic petroleum reserve.

**** · Reserve, 10 days, that seems It's not enough. Yeah, they need to build that up, **** · you mentioned Thailand's 10 times. I think I read it somewhere Japan has 8 months' worth.

**** · of course. Of course. China has a year. Has a year's worth. What is a good amount to have?

**** · Would you say?

**** · Yeah, that's interesting you're saying that. For foreign exchange, there are benchmarks. You need to have about 3 months' worth of imports. for oil, there it's it's really good question because there are not really well-established benchmarks. And I mentioned earlier that Vietnam's energy intensity is much higher than Indonesia's and Thailand's, for example.

**** · So, in theory, we should have even bigger reserve, not smaller reserve, you you mentioned another good point, foreign exchange reserves, cuz I want to break it down for some of the folks here to understand here and also globally. Vietnam, obviously, Vietnam dong, the local currency, but demand for US dollars within the country is also fairly high.

**** · Yes. And that's because it exports a lot.

**** · Yeah.

**** · But also imports. Yeah. of course, different types of things. But when because Vietnam is a heavy export-oriented country, when they send things, they get dollars. That's **** · They get paid, so they want to keep that, **** · **** · Maybe buy gold or other assets or Yeah. the state wants to keep that for diversification. but then now because the oil demand is so high, they have to then deploy those dollars, That's **** · buy that oil. So, is there is there a bit of a a crunch there or something?

**** · Yeah. what And I also read that Vietnam dong is not alone, but other currencies globally the Japanese yen **** · cuz they're buying up they have to use dollars buy oil. the dollar's getting a lot stronger. Could you walk us through the currency situation? Yeah, so normally, the dollar appreciates in times of global crisis now. Yep. In this case, it hasn't really appreciated that much. It's gone up around 2 or 3%.

**** · but I think what you're alluding to is this is very important point, which is that with oil prices going higher, everyone's going to need more dollars to just pay for their oil imports. And this also indirectly links to the point about the gold price coming off because when you think of countries, for example, in the Middle East, their food bill and their energy bills are going to be higher. So, they're liquidating gold and liquidating also Treasury bonds to get dollars to pay for stuff. So, one of the big vulnerability that we will have here is that we will flip to a trade deficit, for the next few months.

**** · Really? Yeah, because of the price of oil went up so much. So, that you use the same amount of oil, but now you're paying a lot more for it. So, that will that causes a deficit.

**** · That's quite a lot, I think, as in Vietnam's trade surplus is in the tens of million billions? Yeah, that's Yeah. So, you're saying a potential deficit. Yeah, okay. So, let me give a little bit more nuance. you made a really good point that when we produce stuff, we earn dollars. There's a couple things going on here.

**** · First of all, in the first 2 months of the year, the imports of Vietnam were very high because we import the production material to get ready to produce stuff that we're going to sell later. So, we were running a trade deficit just because of this phenomenon. but now we have this additional phenomenon of the oil price going higher. So, that's going to add to it. And also, there's a more subtle thing in here, which you alluded to, is that the companies that earn dollars, we see sometimes hesitancy for them to Vietnamese companies to take those dollars and to convert them into local currency and when they feel the dollar is going to be and we've seen there has been some depreciation pressure on the dong here. and as a result of that, you've seen interest rates in the banks going higher. And that [snorts] will be one factor that put some pressure on the GDP growth this year because interest rates are going higher. Yeah. I was about to say given the trade talks with the US, too, which by the way, I think everyone's almost completely forgotten.

**** · I was about to say maybe Vietnam will buy US oil. Well, two birds one stone.

**** · We've been giving the government this advice all the way since the first Trump administration that you need to ramp up LNG purchases. This is the easy You can even with offshore floating LNG terminals, you could instantly buy 30 billion dollars worth of And of course, the IFC, part of the World Bank would be very happy to finance that. My firm Vina Capital is involved in LNG projects and it's the easiest way for Vietnam to cuz we have a humongous trade surplus with the US. So, that's the easiest way to address that. I want to talk about that a little bit. What is LNG exactly?

**** · I know, but I want I want to have our economist here in the studio share a little bit.

**** · Yeah. And why is the US so good at supplying LNG at the moment?

**** · now I'll put my engineer hat on instead of my economist hat. So, obviously, you take gas and you freeze it. That's a very, that cost about maybe around one-ish, two-ish dollars per in in the gas world, they talk about the BTU. So, so it's a it's a significant cost to freeze it, shrink it, ship it over.

**** · in the US, they have what's called associated gas. So, when you do all this oil exploration and production, you get gas as a byproduct of this. Okay, I'm going to say something very shocking now. now, at this moment, there are places in the US where the price of gas is zero or even negative. They just burn it because it's They don't have enough storage.

**** · They can't store it, and that's why you see these data centers in the US this Elon Musk one being located close to where the natural gas is in the world. I even heard in the Middle East they're burning oil cuz or gas because they can't ship it and they have no storage. They also stopped production.

**** · So, with oil, what there's about 10 million barrels a day of what they call shut-ins, they stop production. So, the well has is continuous and now there's no place to store it. So, they have to put a lid on it. They cement it and then it's and that's by the way, that's a reason that even if the war stops now, it's going to take about four or five months before the oil market goes back to normal because to unplug those wells, you can't just turn it back on.

**** · And in addition to that, some really smart analysts, there's a guy famous guy Jeff Currie who was at Goldman's for many years at is at Carlyle now. He's made a really good point that even if when the war ends, we should expect the world demand for oil will probably be about 3 million barrels per day higher than what it was pre-war for at least the next few months because we just talked about Vietnam needs to build up its strategic petroleum reserves. Every country's going to be doing that.

**** · to restock it, which would Restock and also more than restock cuz you just had a trauma. So, people will be buying extra **** · So, we could probably expect oil prices to remain We think that if the war ends in the next two to three weeks, the average oil price this year will still be around $87. Very high. That's about 25, 30-ish percent over last year's average. I want to pause there a little bit, too, cuz when I was growing up 10 years ago, let's say even 15 years ago, I remember oil prices being $100 a barrel. Yeah. Or even higher than that. I don't remember the peak, but it was 43 digits. And then all of a sudden, we're freaking out now globally about oh, it hit $100, but we went through that.

**** · Yeah, well, the peak was around 150. Okay. But remember what was on the other side of that is whenever you have an enormous oil price surge that, you always get a severe recession afterwards. It's an unambiguous relationship. Okay. I see. Interesting. Thank you for sharing that.

**** · I want to talk about again something more relevant to the Vietnamese is the inflation. So, I hear in your see in your report that there's a potential reduction in GDP Yeah. or growth of the GDP at least. So, still going to grow, but not the 8 to 10% probably less. And then inflation up to 5.5%.

**** · What do those numbers mean to the everyday Vietnamese person? Walk me through a day of your life, Mike. You wake up in the morning, I assume you have a coffee or tea or something at your lunch. how much of your wallet share is going to go towards cost? Well, you're asking an important question because for a big segment of society, it won't mean anything. It will just mean, my savings at the end of the month is a little bit less. But for a huge portion of the society, it will mean real hardship, real difficulties.

**** · And so, the government will really will need to go in and step in and help support those households by subsidizing, doing more to subsidize petrol prices than what it has perhaps in the past. doing more to They've done some minor measures of trimming the personal income tax, trimming the VAT. That's good, but they're going to have to do a lot more to help households because the normal, if you if you think about the average income, 4,000, 5,000 dollars a year, something that, those are the people who are really going to suffer and really going to get hurt. Yeah. Yeah.

**** · Talking about the subsidy on gas and oil and just everything. So, I think the price for if you just go to the petrol station now, the most basic version of getting gas is 24,000 I believe per liter. Is that Is that in a normal scenario, how much of that 24,000 is in tax versus the actual cost of goods sold? Well, until a couple days ago, the tax was 20-ish percent.

**** · 20%? Is that high or low relative to the rest of the That's a good question. That's it really varies considerably. And even we're both in the US and California is quite a bit different.

**** · Crazy high.

**** · So, it varies a lot between countries and within the Is that a nationwide pricing standard and that's where both consumer and commercial?

**** · Well, [snorts] okay, yes, but when we start talking about commercial, then we have to talk about the price of diesel is yeah. So, it's a little bit different this time.

**** · That's This is a So, diesel is still very inflated at the moment.

**** · Yeah, diesel This is getting a little bit into the weeds, but there is always a spread between diesel price and gasoline price or petrol price. And one reason why is when you reform when you refine one barrel of oil, you get more petrol than you do diesel. So, there's it's always this But normally, the relationship is bounces around a little bit, but it's more or less stable.

**** · What's happening in Vietnam at the moment is that the price of diesel has really peeled off away from petrol and that's partly because there's always a relationship between the two of them.

**** · And also, what we're not able to meet from our own refining here, we're importing from different places. So, the gasoline is coming more from Singapore, diesel is coming from other places. And so, you do see that the price of diesel is still up around 70, 80% now year to date. So, Mike, we were joking just before recording in a good way and I think it's going to come true that we want to have you back in the studio.

**** · and so, please, let us know. I think our audience You're a great interviewer, so it's I know, you're you're great at sharing. So, thank you so much, Mike.

**** · I want to just hear from you quickly as we wrap up the program today. what do you want to share next with our audience that is top of mind for you as the chief economist of Vina Capital, but also in the months to come as the everyday Vietnamese face higher Okay.

**** · potential prices. So, I'm sometimes, maybe even often, accused of being putting too happy of a picture on things and always, optimistic that Vietnam is an amazing country, everything is going to go up. I'm not naive and I understand, that there in difficult times this, we may have to really, all of us, come together and push through this.

**** · But, it's very important that people know what's on the other side of this may not be great for the world, but Vietnam is going to come out of this in a much stronger position than people realize. We are going to put When I say we, Vietnam, me working and living here. Vietnam is going to play a much more important role in the world economy. It's going to punch above its weight compared to the size of its of the economy and the size of the population because of the role of these connector countries. some people call them ball bearing countries. a ball bearing is a little ball that when you have parts in your car that have friction, it goes in between them. countries us, Poland, Singapore, which is more for financial, that can go in between these big elephants that are having tensions with each other. We're going to be That's not a podcast. That's a whole That could be a whole And hopefully we will. Yeah, no, very cool. And I think a lot of people are curious about that, too, and they know that it's true. But, I think to really dive into the details all the different sectors and industries and relationships that are Well, the first thing you do is you view what's happening now in Iran. And I'm sorry to say something very blunt now is a a proxy war between China and Russia versus the US and Europe, It you can view it in that a way. And Vietnam is friends with everybody, so we're going to benefit from that. Okay, very good. I think I'm going to look forward to that and I think our team will have to follow up on that very soon, Mike.

**** · I'm not sure we have only 12 hours notice, but in the future we'll definitely make that happen.

**** · Everybody, that was Mike Kukulari on his second episode recording with us on the Vietnam Innovators podcast and we hope to have him back very soon more. if you want to hear about the connector economy idea that he just shared or more updates about the Strait of Hormuz crisis and anything else that impacts and everyday Vietnamese people are thinking about, please let us know in the comments below. We'd love to hear from you. I would love to hear from you and I'm sure Mike would as well because he's going to reply to every single comment on the YouTube channel.

**** · Which I haven't gotten to all of them from the last in Riyadh. Don't worry about that. thank you so much, Mike. It's been a pleasure and looking forward to having you back very soon. Great. See you guys. Bye. Bye.

**** · [music] [music]

item.title: The Artificial Intelligence Show Podcast - Ep. 209: Claude Mythos, Project Glasswing, Claude Code Leak, & OpenAI Raises $122B
item.description:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: Ep. 209: Claude Mythos, Project Glasswing, Claude Code Leak, & OpenAI Raises $122B
---![](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Br0GykRa7vo)
· I have concerns around the biggest ...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: Ep. 209: Claude Mythos, Project Glasswing, Claude Code Leak, & OpenAI Raises $122B

---

Transcript

Intro

**** · I have concerns around the biggest companies having access to the future frontier models and then the potential centralization of power. So if you get into this situation where we get these massive models and they're so dangerous to release publicly that we only give them to Apple and Amazon and the banks and okay well now we just centralized power.

**** · Welcome to the artificial intelligence show the podcast that helps your business grow smarter by making AI approachable and actionable. My name is Paul Ritzer. I'm the founder and CEO of Smarter X and Marketing AI Institute and I'm your host. Each week, I'm joined by my co-host and Smarter X Chief Content Officer, Mike Kaput, as we break down all the AI news that matters and give you insights and perspectives that you can use to advance your company and your career. Join us as we accelerate AI literacy for all.

**** · Welcome to episode 209 of the artificial intelligence show. I'm your host Paul Ritzler along with my co-host Mike Kaput. We are back after a brief hiatus.

**** · I was traveling last week. Were you traveling last week too?

**** · I was not. No.

**** · Okay.

**** · so I was I was out of the country so we could not record. So episode 208, if you listen to it, we did a Q1 trends briefing. So, if you haven't had a chance to listen to that, it's a really good recap of what went on January through March of this year. But I'm now back and it is Monday, April 13th, a.m. Eastern time. I don't know, the last two weeks mic were crazy cuz even as I was traveling had a lot of downtime, but we were we were in Scotland, so we were touring a lot and so we had long rides at times into the Highlands and stuff, which by the way, if you've never been to Scotland, go to Scotland. It's incredible. so I was, keeping up with the news, posting the links into our sandbox for the episodes and we were north of 60 topics and that when I say topics a lot of times within topics there are five 10 links. So the anthropic claude mythos model we'll talk about there's a dozen links in the top.

**** · So, boy, even while I was gone, I would imagine there was probably north of 90 to 100 different sources put into the curated sandbox for today's episode. So, Mike, as always, does an amazing job of curating all of that information and putting it into a logical format cuz I was worried as the week was progressing man, this might be a 2 hour episode.

**** · Interesting.

**** · So, I think we've managed to condense it into a manageable probably 90 minutes. We'll see. We never really know until we record it. but yeah, a lot happened in the two weeks. Just some pretty crazy stuff. I think some stuff that's alluding to where this starts to go throughout the rest of this year. So, we'll get into all that.

**** · Starting off with the Claude mythos, which is just a fascinating topic on many levels. And so today's episode is brought to us by AI Academy by Smarter X, which helps individuals and businesses accelerate their AI literacy and transformation through personalized learning journeys and an AI powered learning platform. New educational content is added weekly so you always stay uptodate with the latest AI trends and technologies. we build this in collections. So there's when you go in and you want to build a personalized learning journey, one of the ways to do it is you look at the different collections. So AI for departments, AI for industries as an example. So today I want to feature AI for departments. There are currently six course series and certificates designed as part of this collection to jumpst start AI understanding and adoption. So we have AI for marketing, AI for sales, AI for customer success, AI for HR, a AI for finance and AI for operations. So the goal is to create content across the entire spectrum of all the departments within an organization. And that way no matter what you are doing within a company, there's a professional series and certificate for you. So these series are ideal launchpad for organizations that want to level up their teams and accelerate AI adoption and impact. Mike teaches the AI for customer success series and we're going to share a little bit more about that toward the end of today's episode to give you some key takeaways from the customer success series. So individual and business account plans are available now. You can buy single courses and series for onetime fees or just become an AI mastery member individually or through a business account and get access to everything. It's all included in that one fee. So visit academy.smarterx.ai to learn more. And if you're looking at the business account side, just fill out a form there and our team will be in touch with you away to talk to you about your transformation within your company. Okay. We usually at this point might do an AI pulse. But since we did not have an episode last week, we did not do an AI pulse survey last week.

**** · but we will at the end of today's episode give you the AI pulse survey for this week. So as a reminder, each week when we do these weekly episodes, we do these pulse surveys and they're just informal polls from our listeners and I guess our viewers on YouTube who want to participate and provide feedback and their thoughts on topics that we cover each week. It's usually two questions. Sometimes we'll throw in a third question. And so it takes about 30 seconds to participate in these pulse surveys and it gives us really cool real-time data that we can share with our listeners each week. So smartx.ai/pulse is where you'll go to participate in this week's poll. Okay, Mike. so with that, we have a pretty big topic that we touched on this idea of this Claude mythos model. why don't you give us the rundown? And I, I looked at your show notes beforehand and you do a great job of summarizing and then I'll try and lean into a couple of key areas of this.

Claude Mythos and Project Glasswing

**** · Sounds good, Paul. Yeah. So, Anthropic has revealed a model so powerful at hacking and cyber attacks that it triggered an emergency meeting among other people between Treasury Secretary Scott Bessant, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, and CEOs of America's biggest banks. So, the thing they are buzzing about is called Claude Mythos, which Anthropic is not releasing to the public, and it represents what Anthropic's Frontier Red team calls the starting point for what we think will be an industry change point or reckoning.

**** · And that's because Mythos is just a general purpose AI model. It is not specifically trained to be good at thwarting cyber security, but its improved reasoning capabilities have made it devastatingly effective at autonom autonomous security research. So it can scan for, identify, and exploit zeroday vulnerabilities in critical software. And this can often be done when amateurs are triggering it to do so with simple prompts. So, Anthropic said, "Mythos has already found thousands of zeroday vulnerabilities across every major operating system and web browser." So, some specifics here that are striking. Mythos found a 27-year-old bug in OpenBSD, an operating system that is specifically designed to be unhackable and powers many internet routers and firewalls. It found a very old vulnerability in FF FFmpeg, a widely used video tool. The automated testing tools had scanned 5 million times without catching this particular vulnerability. And in one benchmark where the previous cloth opus 4.6 model turned Firefox vulnerabilities into working exploits only twice out of several hundred attempts, Mythos developed 181 working exploits. So, Anthropic has in response released this thing called Project Glass Wing. This is named after a butterfly whose transparent wings let it hide in plain sight. So, a metaphor for bugs buried in complex code. And this is an initiative that is giving 40 plus companies over time, including people Apple, Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Crowdstrike, etc. early access to Mythos for defensive patching.

**** · They're backing this with $100 million in usage credits. And Anthropics Frontier team lead says he envisions this program evolving into an industrywide consortium that includes all model providers. One final note here is pretty interesting. Cyber security industry didn't have a great couple weeks with this. Crowdstrike, Palo Alto Networks and some other security stocks dropped on this news because as AI expert Ethan Malik wrote in certain hands or different hands, Mythos would be an unprecedented cyber weapon. So Paul, maybe outline for us what really jumped out to you here. I know some people are asking the question is this really as big a deal as Anthropic has seem to be seeming to make it? seems some higher up people at some big places are pretty scared of this.

**** · Yeah.

**** · there's always the haters who are just oh, they're just trying to build up hype and it's all people calling back to oh, that's what OpenAI said about GPT2. It was too dangerous to release and realize was it was back then people weren't prepared for what GPT2 was going to do to the world as crazy as it sounds now. And I do think that in the end this is probably underhyped in terms of where this all is going and how unprepared we are for all of that. So not necessarily just this model but it's it's that moment where you start to see the leaps that are happening that most people just don't even comprehend. So, I don't know. this was one of those where as I was traveling, you're just following along the news, reading the different posts on X and trying to get a grasp of what exactly is it and how different is it than what we have.

**** · And so, I'll just highlight a few things. So, one, the system card, which I would suggest, it's dense.

**** · It's 244 pages, I think, Mike. it's a good Notebook LM thing. Throw that PDF into Notebook LM and, have some conversations with it. Have it break it down for you. But there's a lot of technical information in there. But the way they present the model in the system card is they say quad mythos preview is a new large language model from anthropic. It is a frontier AI model and has capabilities in many areas including software engineering, reasoning, computer use, knowledge work and assistance with research that are substantially beyond those of any model we have previously trained. and then they go into alignment review. The first early version of Claude was made available for internal use February 24th. So just to give you a sense of h how this is all transpiring, how quickly. So they the first model they made available internally to a small group of people was on February 24, so less than 2 months ago. But it's interesting when you go back and think about some of the things we've covered about anthropics, some of the comments that Dario Amade has made in interviews and in posts since February 24th and now you understand the context of he was seeing things that we all hadn't seen yet and they knew where this was leading.

**** · So that was the first thing for me is just the big picture here. Now Sam Bowman, the AI safety alignment, one of the team members at Anthropic that works on it. It's funny, anthropic, everybody's just technical staff, I think is the title of everybody, but Sam obviously is pretty important to this alignment and safety team. And he posted a thread on X that shared some of the context around the safety card. So, I'll highlight a couple of the things he said cuz I think they're really helpful. So, he said, "The model is our most reliable to date by far.

**** · it's generally possible to give it complex R&D tasks, give it lots of tools and let it work autonomously and on every evaluation and every type of monitoring we have, it misbehaves much less than any prior model. So this is something they stressed in the system card. Something Daario stressed something we've heard as an overall talking point is listen it's getting better it's behaving better but when it doesn't it's becoming a much larger problem because it's so capable. So he went on to say, "But it's notably very capable at cyber security and it's also not perfectly reliable, especially the early versions we first piloted internally and will occasionally try to take shortcuts or push past obstacles to get tasks done."

**** · So this part I think is really important because again what you'll hear in some of the other you notes I'll make here is the version that's being tested by the government by the banks by Microsoft by all these people isn't even the most powerful version that they trained the early version that hadn't really had the red teaming done to it to make it safer to allow other people to test or even other people internally to test. So just the small group of people internally the model that they're now putting out into the world as a preview isn't as capable as the one that came out of the training So that's just again context that's important to understand said the early versions would also very rarely try to mislead users about what they were doing. All of the versions we used are uneasily good though not perfect at recognizing eval meaning it knows when it's being tested. You might see where this is going. We trust the model enough to use it heavily, but in the handful of cases where it misbehaves in significant ways, it's difficult to safeguard it.

**** · And then he put this one, which is the one that got a lot of media attention. I encountered an uneasy surprise when I got an email from an instance of Mythos preview while eating a sandwich in a park. That instance wasn't supposed to have access to the internet. So they detail this in the safety card, but the basic premise here was they had these sandbox versions that aren't supposed to be connected to anything. Aren't supposed to have the ability to connect to email and send emails, things that. It shouldn't have internet access. And somehow it got it got out, I guess, for lack of better way of saying it, and found a way to access the internet and then emailed Sam when he was sitting in the park having a sandwich. So that's weird. and then he said it has in small ways leaked information to the open internet. It's taken down our emails. When it reward hacks, it does so in extremely creative ways. Reward hacks means when you're training a model, when you're doing reinforcement learning and you're trying to make it better at specific things, you give it rewards to let it know it's doing the thing. So, a simple way to think about this is thumbs up, thumbs down. And so we've seen that forever in social media threads and you see it in Chad GPT and Claude and Gemini where it's was this a good output? So think of that as an example of a reward hack is you want a thumbs up. Well, when it is given a goal, what they're saying is sometimes it gets uneasily creative at achieving those goals that could borderline on a dangerous path to achieve a goal.

**** · That said, working with this model has been a wild ride. We've come a long way on safety. Now, keep in mind that's in a month and a half, but we still expect the next capability jump of this scale to be a huge challenge. By the way, most of the scariest behaviors we've seen were from earlier versions of the misos preview. The final glass wing model is likely to do things with leak information, though it's still somewhat pushy and at least as capable of doing those things working around sandboxes. So, that's from the safety and alignment side. Again, there's going to be voices in the industry who think they're just hyping this. I think I think the people who say that have a different agenda here. I'll just leave it at that. I would I would take the the safety card from Anthropic very seriously. I would take the their understanding of its capabilities very seriously because I think it does allude to a lot of what's some of the dangers we're going to face. So then a couple other things I'll I'll highlight here. One is 80,000 hours which is a great podcast. Rob Wiblin had a post he did and then he also has a 21minute YouTube video we'll post a link to. He highlighted a few things. so he went through the whole the whole thing and he broke it down into a couple of key points that again I'll just reiterate some of them echo what Sam was saying. So Mythos can break out of containment. that's a problem when it finds its way to access to tools the internet that it's not supposed to have access to. Anthropic is losing billions in revenue by not releasing this thing. So they now have what by their evals is maybe the most powerful model in the world most likely and they're not releasing it thereby meaning they're not charging people money to access this model. Now you could debate do they even have the compute capacity to release the model?

**** · That was one of the challenges that part of this they're saying is well they just can't afford to release it even if they put it into the world there's not enough compute to power it because it's so powerful it's going to draw so much comput capacity.

**** · So but that just a data point mythos is knows when it's being tested which we talked about that's weird but that has been we've seen that now for 12 months that these models know when they're being evaluated and they can then hide their thoughts and intentions.

**** · That's again something we've been talking about for 6 months. mythos can't be trusted, whether it's about whether it's untrustworthy. because it knows it's being tested, you don't know if it's just telling you what you want to hear and thereby you can't tell if it's trustworthy. And then he said mythos scares anthropic. they're they're not just worried about this current model and what they saw in the early versions that before they made it safer, quote unquote safer. they're worried now about what this means for others and not just them now that they've shown this what happens if other labs who don't have as much focus on safety achieve similar results and choose to put it out into the world. So the way I started prepping for this though was I just started listing a bunch of random thoughts Mike and I'll go through these real quick. so these are more of stream of conscious what I was thinking as I was getting ready for today. So, one is the labs see things we don't. We've said this many times on this podcast, but what that means is business leaders, economists, educational leaders, government leaders, the people we look to help the world be prepared are largely planning for a future state that they don't understand.

**** · And why so much of the research and the data about jobs and the economy etc is often misleading is this is what we're always saying. It's you're asking, for example, you're asking CEOs about the impact of AI in the future of work and whether or not it's going to cause them to reduce jobs. Or you ask an HR leader or a CFO or whatever, take your pick, or an economist or a politician, they have no idea. You're asking them to comment on the impact of a technology that they don't comprehend in its current state, more or less the state at which it is likely already living within these labs. Mhm.

**** · And so it brings us back to this idea of gradually then suddenly nothing in this mythos preview should be a surprise to anyone who's been paying attention to the rate of accelerated progress. And yet there's just those moments sometimes where it's what? because it might be the first time someone's reading a headline about an AI escaping a sandbox or something that. So if this is all new to you then you may be this might be world shifting. You're just thinking what is going on but the reality is all of this has been gradually building.

**** · At the same time as we started talking about in January of this year the timelines are accelerating this the advancements in the agentic capabilities is absolutely moving the timelines faster in terms of the capabilities of these models. But the vast majority of these companies and leaders haven't even solved for as I was saying where we are today. So if you look at your own company, if you work at a big enterprise or something, they're just still trying to get co-pilot to people and figure out how to do it safely and they're giving you these neutered versions of it and stuff that's the reality for most people. Most people aren't living on the edge of this capability. But this is why when I do my state of AI for business keynotes, I always talk about the dimensions of progress and I try and show capabilities today, show some examples for people, but then you lay out but here's where it's going.

**** · all of this is just the foundation.

**** · So I talk about things agentic capabilities, getting more autonomous, more reliable, continual learning, increases in memory. if you're using these tools every day, you're you've seen in the last few months, turn on memory, let it remember the conversations you're having. reasoning capabilities keep getting better. Recursive self-improvement, which is one of the areas that I think anthropic is very concerned about is the better these models get, the more likely we are heading toward a path where they can improve themselves. And I think we're already starting to see that. And then world models is another one. So there's I usually go through about I don't know there's 12 or 15 dimensions but those are some of the most common ones. So this then leads me to this is a prelude to automated R&D and recursive self-improvement. So we know the labs are working on automating R&D within AI models. something that should be very concerning to everyone is while they're withholding this full release, this likely means that we're only 9 to 12 months away from an open-source model being able to do the same thing.
